cell_specs.implementation_plan: Environment setup cell. (1) Import numpy as np, matplotlib, matplotlib.pyplot as plt, and ipywidgets as widgets; also import IPython.display.display. Wrap the ipywidgets import in a try/except so a missing widget backend does not abort the notebook: on ImportError set a module-level flag WIDGETS_AVAILABLE=False and print a warning, else WIDGETS_AVAILABLE=True. (2) Define set_seed(seed=0) that calls np.random.seed(seed) and returns seed. (3) Define softmax(x, axis=-1): subtract np.max(x, axis=axis, keepdims=True) for numerical stability, exponentiate, divide by np.sum(exp, axis=axis, keepdims=True). Operate on float64. (4) Rely on the inline backend (Colab/Jupyter default); do not call get_ipython() magics in raw Python. Set plt.rcParams figure size to a legible default. (5) Print a one-line confirmation listing numpy and matplotlib versions so the cell produces visible output under Run-all.
state_variables: WIDGETS_AVAILABLE: bool flag set during ipywidgets import
np, plt, widgets, display module handles used by all later cells
error_handling: try/except ImportError around ipywidgets; set WIDGETS_AVAILABLE=False and print a warning instead of raising
softmax subtracts the per-axis max before exp to avoid overflow on large scores
test_checks: print('Imports OK | numpy', np.__version__, '| matplotlib', matplotlib.__version__)

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Define the single shared toy problem and document the matrix-shape convention once, in a top comment block: inputs are stored COLUMN-WISE, I has shape [d, N] where column j is input vector a^j; projections map d->d_k (or d->d_v). Steps: (1) set_seed(0). (2) TOKENS = ['the', 'cat', 'sat', 'down']; N = len(TOKENS). (3) Choose d = 4 (model dim) and d_k = 3 (key/query dim); set d_v = d_k and document that O therefore has shape [d_v, N]. (4) Build I as np.random.randn(d, N) cast to float64. (5) Build W_q, W_k each np.random.randn(d_k, d) * 0.5 and W_v np.random.randn(d_v, d) * 0.5 so q/k/v stay small. (6) Print shapes of I, W_q, W_k, W_v and the TOKENS so the shared state is visible. Expose I, TOKENS, W_q, W_k, W_v, d, d_k, d_v, N as module-level names consumed by every later code cell.
state_variables: TOKENS: list[str] length N
N: int sequence length
d: int model dimension, d_k: int key dim, d_v: int value dim
I: np.ndarray shape [d, N] (columns are input vectors)
W_q: [d_k, d], W_k: [d_k, d], W_v: [d_v, d]
error_handling: assert d_k <= d and d_v == d_k to keep the shared convention consistent for later cells
assert I.shape == (d, N) immediately after construction so any later shape bug surfaces here
test_checks: assert W_q.shape == (d_k, d) and W_k.shape == (d_k, d) and W_v.shape == (d_v, d)
print I.shape, W_q.shape, W_k.shape, W_v.shape and TOKENS

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Compute one output vector b^1 (0-indexed column 0) step by step. (1) Project the whole sequence: Q = W_q @ I, K = W_k @ I, V = W_v @ I, giving Q,K shape [d_k, N] and V shape [d_v, N]; column j holds q^j, k^j, v^j. (2) Take q1 = Q[:, 0]. (3) Raw scores: raw_scores = K.T @ q1 (equivalently np.dot(q1, K[:, j]) for each j), shape [N]. (4) Scaled scores: scaled = raw_scores / np.sqrt(d_k). (5) Weights: alpha1 = softmax(scaled, axis=-1) over the N keys. (6) Output: b1 = V @ alpha1, shape [d_v]. (7) Print, with token labels, q1, every k^j, raw_scores, scaled scores, alpha1 (sums to 1), and b1, formatted with np.round so students can trace each number. Expose Q, K, V, b1, alpha1 for reuse by C06 and C08.
state_variables: Q: [d_k, N], K: [d_k, N], V: [d_v, N] projected matrices
q1, raw_scores, scaled, alpha1, b1 intermediates
error_handling: assert Q.shape == (d_k, N) and V.shape == (d_v, N) right after projection
guard the scale: scale = np.sqrt(d_k); assert scale > 0
test_checks: print round(alpha1, 4) and round(b1, 4) with TOKENS labels

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Interactive single-position attention demo over the shared Q, K, V. (1) Define attention_for_position(i): given 0-based query index i, compute q_i = Q[:, i], scores = (K.T @ q_i) / np.sqrt(d_k), alpha = softmax(scores, axis=-1), b_i = V @ alpha; return alpha, b_i. (2) Define plot_position(i): create a matplotlib figure, draw a bar chart of alpha over range(N) with TOKENS as xticklabels, title 'Attention weights for query position i=<i> ("<token>")', ylabel 'alpha_prime[i,j]', annotate each bar with its value, print b_i rounded, then plt.show(). (3) Default invocation: call plot_position(0) directly so Run-all renders output before any interaction. (4) If WIDGETS_AVAILABLE: build an IntSlider(min=0, max=N-1, value=0, description='query i') (or Dropdown over TOKENS) wired via widgets.interactive_output into an Output area calling plot_position. If widgets unavailable, skip widget wiring (default plot already rendered).
state_variables: reuses Q, K, V, d_k, TOKENS, N from earlier cells
optional slider/dropdown widget and Output area
error_handling: clamp/assert 0 <= i < N at the top of attention_for_position
guard widget construction behind WIDGETS_AVAILABLE so non-interactive Run-all still succeeds
create a fresh figure and call plt.show() inside plot_position to avoid duplicate axes on repeated slider events
test_checks: default plot_position(0) produces a visible bar chart under Run-all

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Vectorized self-attention in one pass and equivalence assertion against C05. (1) Recompute Q = W_q @ I, K = W_k @ I, V = W_v @ I. (2) A = (K.T @ Q) / np.sqrt(d_k), shape [N, N]; document that A[j, i] is the score of key j against query i, so attention for query i lives in COLUMN i. (3) A_prime = softmax(A, axis=0) so each query column (over keys j) sums to 1. (4) O = V @ A_prime, shape [d_v, N]; column i is b^i. (5) Verify: assert np.allclose(O[:, 0], b1) and assert np.allclose(A_prime[:, 0], alpha1), proving per-element and matrix views match. (6) Print A, A_prime (rounded), confirm column sums are 1, print a success message. Expose A, A_prime, O for C09/C11/C13.
state_variables: A: [N, N] scaled scores, A_prime: [N, N] column-softmaxed, O: [d_v, N] outputs
reuses W_q, W_k, W_v, I, d_k, b1, alpha1 from earlier cells
error_handling: assert A.shape == (N, N) and O.shape == (d_v, N)
use np.allclose with explicit atol (e.g. 1e-8) rather than == when comparing to C05
if the equivalence assert fails, print both vectors so the convention mismatch is diagnosable
test_checks: print('Matrix view matches per-element b^1:', True)

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Interactive attention-matrix heatmap showing the role of scaling/temperature. (1) Define recompute_scores(): raw = K.T @ Q (unscaled, [N, N]); return raw. (2) Define plot_heatmap(apply_scaling: bool, temperature: float): start from raw; divide by np.sqrt(d_k) if apply_scaling; divide by max(temperature, 1e-6); A_vis = softmax(scores, axis=0). Render with plt.imshow(A_vis, aspect='auto'), add colorbar, set xticks/yticks to range(N) with TOKENS labels (xlabel 'query', ylabel 'key'), title noting scaling on/off and temperature, annotate cells with rounded weights, plt.show(). (3) Default invocation: plot_heatmap(apply_scaling=True, temperature=1.0). (4) If WIDGETS_AVAILABLE: Checkbox(value=True, description='apply 1/sqrt(d_k)') and FloatSlider(min=0.1, max=3.0, step=0.1, value=1.0, description='temperature') wired via interactive_output into an Output area. Print pedagogical note: less scaling / lower temperature sharpens attention toward one-hot.
state_variables: raw unscaled score matrix [N, N]
reuses Q, K, d_k, TOKENS, N; optional Checkbox + FloatSlider + Output
error_handling: clamp temperature to a small positive floor (max(temperature, 1e-6)) to avoid divide-by-zero
guard widgets behind WIDGETS_AVAILABLE; default heatmap renders regardless
create a fresh figure and plt.show() inside plot_heatmap to prevent figure accumulation on slider events
test_checks: assert np.allclose(A_vis.sum(axis=0), 1.0) for the default render
print scaled-vs-unscaled max column weight to show they differ
default plot_heatmap(True, 1.0) yields a visible heatmap under Run-all

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Multi-head self-attention, parameterizable by head count h (default 2). (1) Define multi_head_attention(h): require d_k % h == 0; head_dim = d_k // h (and d_v // h for values). Split Q, K, V along the feature axis into h slices of head_dim rows each (Q_head = Q[head*head_dim:(head+1)*head_dim, :], etc.). (2) Per head: A_head = (K_head.T @ Q_head) / np.sqrt(head_dim); A_head_prime = softmax(A_head, axis=0); O_head = V_head @ A_head_prime, shape [head_dim, N]; collect A_head_prime and O_head. (3) Concatenate O_heads along axis 0 -> O_concat shape [d_v, N]. (4) set_seed for reproducibility then W_O = np.random.randn(d_v, d_v) * 0.5; O_multi = W_O @ O_concat. (5) Return list of per-head A_prime and O_multi. (6) Define plot_heads(h): compute, then draw an h-panel subplot grid (plt.subplots(1, h)) of per-head attention heatmaps with TOKENS labels and a shared title, plt.show(). (7) Default: plot_heads(2). (8) If WIDGETS_AVAILABLE: Dropdown/IntSlider restricted to divisors of d_k wired to plot_heads. Assert O_multi.shape == O.shape from C08.
state_variables: head_dim, per-head Q/K/V slices, per-head A_prime list, O_concat, W_O, O_multi
reuses Q, K, V, d_k, d_v, TOKENS, N, set_seed; optional IntSlider/Dropdown + Output
error_handling: assert d_k % h == 0 (raise a clear ValueError naming valid divisors if not)
restrict the widget to head counts that evenly divide d_k so an invalid split is impossible
assert O_multi.shape == O.shape to confirm multi-head preserves output dimensionality
guard widgets behind WIDGETS_AVAILABLE; fresh figure + plt.show() inside plot_heads
test_checks: default plot_heads(2) renders two heatmap panels under Run-all

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Sinusoidal positional encoding plus a permutation-equivariance demonstration. (1) Define positional_encoding(seq_len, d): build PE shape [d, seq_len] (column = position) using PE[2i, pos]=sin(pos/10000**(2i/d)), PE[2i+1, pos]=cos(pos/10000**(2i/d)); handle odd d by truncating the last cos term. (2) Define run_attention(I_in): Q=W_q@I_in, K=W_k@I_in, V=W_v@I_in, A=(K.T@Q)/sqrt(d_k), A'=softmax(A, axis=0), O=V@A'; return O. (3) Permutation demo: build perm (e.g. reversed range(N)); compute O = run_attention(I) and show WITHOUT PE that run_attention(I[:, perm]) equals O[:, perm] (permutation-equivariant) via np.allclose; then I_pe = I + positional_encoding(N, d) and show that run_attention(I_pe)[:, perm] != run_attention(I_pe[:, perm]), so adding e^i makes order matter. Print before/after comparison and the boolean results. (4) Define plot_pe(seq_len, add_pe): imshow positional_encoding(seq_len, d) (xlabel position, ylabel dim) with colorbar; title reflects add_pe; plt.show(). (5) Default: plot_pe(N, True). (6) If WIDGETS_AVAILABLE: IntSlider(min=2, max=16, value=N, description='seq_len') and Checkbox(value=True, description='add positional encoding') wired via interactive_output.
state_variables: PE: [d, seq_len] sinusoidal table
perm: permutation index array; O, I_pe intermediates
reuses W_q, W_k, W_v, I, d, d_k, N, softmax, TOKENS; optional IntSlider+Checkbox+Output
error_handling: handle odd d in positional_encoding by guarding the cos index (slice to available rows)
use np.allclose (atol ~1e-8) for the equivariance comparison rather than ==
clamp seq_len slider to >= 1; PE columns sliced to the requested seq_len
guard widgets behind WIDGETS_AVAILABLE; fresh figure + plt.show() inside plot_pe
test_checks: assert np.allclose(run_attention(I)[:, perm], run_attention(I[:, perm])) is True (equivariant without PE)
assert not np.allclose(run_attention(I + positional_encoding(N, d))[:, perm], run_attention((I + positional_encoding(N, d))[:, perm])) (PE breaks equivariance)
default plot_pe(N, True) renders the PE heatmap under Run-all

cross_cell_invariants: Matrix-shape convention is fixed in C03 and never changed: inputs are column-wise, I is [d, N] with column j = a^j; Q=W_q@I and K=W_k@I are [d_k, N]; V=W_v@I is [d_v, N]; the attention matrix A=K.T@Q is [N, N] and is softmaxed over axis=0 (keys) so each query column sums to 1; O=V@A' is [d_v, N] with column i = b^i.
All randomness is seeded via set_seed() (defined in C02, first called in C03) so I, W_q, W_k, W_v, and W_O are identical across reruns and every cell shares the same numbers.
softmax() from C02 is the only softmax used; scaling is always 1/sqrt(d_k) for single-head and 1/sqrt(head_dim) for multi-head.
d_v == d_k (set in C03) so single-head O (C08) and multi-head O (C11) share shape [d_v, N] and can be compared by shape.

Every interactive cell (C06, C09, C11, C13) calls its plotting function once with default arguments BEFORE building any widget, so a non-interactive Run-all (Stage 5 verification) always produces visible output; widget construction is guarded by WIDGETS_AVAILABLE.
The per-element result b^1 / alpha1 from C05 is the ground truth that the matrix view in C08 asserts against; downstream cells (C09, C11, C13) build on C08's A/A'/O.
assumptions: NumPy + matplotlib (+ ipywidgets for interactivity) only; small synthetic sequence (N=4, d=4, d_k=d_v=3) runs in seconds on a free Colab CPU runtime with no downloads or API keys.
ipywidgets may be unavailable under the headless Stage 5 kernel; cells degrade gracefully via the WIDGETS_AVAILABLE flag and a default non-interactive render.
Target runtime is Google Colab (Python 3.12, numpy 2.0.2); matplotlib uses the inline backend without explicit get_ipython() magics in the Python source.
Audience knows basic linear algebra and softmax; printed intermediates reinforce rather than re-teach these.
Exact toy dimensions (d, d_k, N) and token labels are chosen for legibility and may be adjusted in C03 as long as the cross-cell invariants (column-wise convention, d_v==d_k, seeded randomness) hold.
